annotations.label: Polynomial

anchor.snippet: a polynomial is a mathematical expression consisting of indeterminates

annotations.note: `Polynomial R` is defined as `R[X]`, a structure wrapping a finitely-supported coefficient function.

annotations.label: Variable / indeterminate

anchor.snippet: is commonly called a variable or an indeterminate

annotations.note: `Polynomial.X` is the indeterminate of the univariate polynomial ring.

annotations.label: Evaluation by substitution

anchor.snippet: denotes, by convention, the result of substituting

annotations.note: `Polynomial.eval` substitutes a ring element for the indeterminate.

annotations.label: Substituting the indeterminate yields the polynomial

anchor.snippet: does not change anything

annotations.note: `Polynomial.comp_X : p.comp X = p` states that substituting the indeterminate returns the polynomial unchanged.

annotations.label: Polynomial (built from constants and variables)

anchor.snippet: A polynomial is an expression that can be built from constants and symbols called variables

annotations.note: `R[X]` is generated by constants (`Polynomial.C`) and the variable (`Polynomial.X`) under ring operations.

annotations.label: Equivalent polynomial expressions

anchor.snippet: are two polynomial expressions that represent the same polynomial

annotations.note: An illustrative example of polynomial-expression equality, not a named Mathlib statement.

annotations.label: Standard form of a univariate polynomial

anchor.snippet: A polynomial in a single indeterminate x can always be written

annotations.note: `Polynomial.as_sum_range` writes `p = ∑ i in range (natDegree+1), monomial i (coeff p i)`, the standard form.

annotations.label: Leading coefficient

anchor.snippet: The leading coefficient of such a polynomial is

annotations.note: `Polynomial.leadingCoeff` is the coefficient of the highest-degree term.

annotations.label: Polynomial as finite sum of terms

anchor.snippet: a polynomial can either be zero or can be written as the sum of a finite number of non-zero terms

annotations.note: `Polynomial.as_sum_support` expresses `p` as a finite sum of monomials over its (finite) support.

annotations.label: Degree of a term and of a polynomial

anchor.snippet: The exponent on an indeterminate in a term is called the degree of that indeterminate in that term

annotations.note: `Polynomial.degree` gives the polynomial degree; `Polynomial.degree_monomial` gives a term's degree.

annotations.label: Constant term and constant polynomial

anchor.snippet: A term with no indeterminates and a polynomial with no indeterminates are called, respectively, a constant term and a constant polynomial

annotations.note: `Polynomial.C` embeds the coefficient ring as constant polynomials; the constant term of `p` is `p.coeff 0`.

annotations.label: Degree of a term

anchor.snippet: is a term. The coefficient is

annotations.note: A worked numeric example instantiating the general `Polynomial.degree_monomial`.

annotations.label: Polynomial as sum of three terms

anchor.snippet: Forming a sum of several terms produces a polynomial.

annotations.note: A specific example illustrating that finite sums of monomials are polynomials, covered by `as_sum_support`.

annotations.label: Linear, quadratic, cubic polynomials

anchor.snippet: Polynomials of degree one, two or three are respectively linear polynomials

annotations.note: Degree is formalized via `Polynomial.degree`, but there are no named `linear`/`quadratic`/`cubic` polynomial definitions.

annotations.label: Zero polynomial

anchor.snippet: is called the zero polynomial

annotations.note: The zero polynomial is `(0 : R[X])` from the additive structure of `Polynomial`.

annotations.label: Homogeneous polynomial

anchor.snippet: a polynomial is called homogeneous of degree

annotations.note: `MvPolynomial.IsHomogeneous φ n` holds when all monomials of `φ` have total degree `n`.

annotations.label: Like terms

anchor.snippet: Two terms with the same indeterminates raised to the same powers are called

annotations.note: "Like terms" is an informal collection notion with no corresponding Mathlib declaration.

annotations.label: Monomial, binomial, trinomial

anchor.snippet: a one-term polynomial is called a monomial

annotations.note: `Polynomial.monomial` is formalized, but `binomial`/`trinomial` have no named definitions.

annotations.label: Real, integer, complex polynomials

anchor.snippet: A real polynomial is a polynomial with real coefficients.

annotations.note: Real/integer/complex polynomials are `Polynomial ℝ`, `Polynomial ℤ`, `Polynomial ℂ`.

annotations.label: Univariate and multivariate polynomials

anchor.snippet: A polynomial in one indeterminate is called a univariate polynomial

annotations.note: Univariate is `Polynomial` and multivariate is `MvPolynomial σ R`.

annotations.label: Addition of polynomials

anchor.snippet: Polynomials can be added using the associative law of addition

annotations.note: `R[X]` has an additive structure with `coeff_add` giving coefficientwise addition.

annotations.label: Multiplication of polynomials

anchor.snippet: Polynomials can also be multiplied.

annotations.note: `Polynomial.coeff_mul` gives the Cauchy-product formula for polynomial multiplication.

annotations.label: Composition of polynomials

anchor.snippet: is obtained by substituting each copy of the variable of the first polynomial by the second polynomial

annotations.note: `Polynomial.comp p q` substitutes `q` for the variable of `p`.

annotations.label: Division and rational fractions

anchor.snippet: The division of one polynomial by another is not typically a polynomial.

annotations.note: `RatFunc K` is the field of rational fractions, quotients of polynomials.

annotations.label: Euclidean division of polynomials

anchor.snippet: there is a notion of Euclidean division of polynomials

annotations.note: `Polynomial.modByMonic`/`divByMonic` with `modByMonic_add_div` give Euclidean division with remainder of smaller degree.

annotations.label: Polynomial remainder theorem

anchor.snippet: the polynomial remainder theorem asserts that the remainder of the division

annotations.note: `p %ₘ (X - C a) = C (p.eval a)` is exactly the remainder theorem.

annotations.label: Factorization into irreducible polynomials

anchor.snippet: also have a factored form in which the polynomial is written as a product of irreducible polynomials and a constant

annotations.note: `D[X]` is a `UniqueFactorizationMonoid`, giving factorization into irreducibles times a unit.

annotations.label: Derivative of a polynomial

anchor.snippet: The derivative of the polynomial

annotations.note: `Polynomial.derivative` is the formal derivative, shown to agree with the analytic derivative.

annotations.label: Antiderivative of a polynomial

anchor.snippet: the general antiderivative (or indefinite integral)

annotations.note: No formal polynomial antiderivative/indefinite-integral operation is defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Formal derivative over a ring

anchor.snippet: the formula for the derivative can still be interpreted formally

annotations.note: `Polynomial.derivative` is defined formally over any commutative semiring, independent of analysis.

annotations.label: Polynomial function

anchor.snippet: A polynomial function is a function defined by evaluating a polynomial.

annotations.note: The polynomial function is `fun x => p.eval x` via `Polynomial.eval`.

annotations.label: Polynomial function of one variable

anchor.snippet: is a polynomial function of one variable

annotations.note: A specific example of evaluating a univariate polynomial, an instance of `Polynomial.eval`.

annotations.label: Polynomial functions are continuous, smooth, entire

anchor.snippet: Every polynomial function is continuous

annotations.note: `Polynomial.continuous` proves continuity; smoothness/entirety follow from `Polynomial.hasStrictDerivAt` and contDiff results.

annotations.label: Evaluation of a polynomial

anchor.snippet: The evaluation of a polynomial is the computation of the corresponding polynomial function

annotations.note: `Polynomial.eval` computes the value of the polynomial function at a point.

annotations.label: Horner's method

anchor.snippet: using Horner's method

annotations.note: `Polynomial.recOnHorner` provides the Horner-form recursion structure, but Mathlib has no Horner evaluation algorithm proper.

annotations.label: Graph of zero polynomial

anchor.snippet: The graph of the zero polynomial

annotations.note: Geometric graph descriptions of polynomials are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Graph of degree 0 polynomial

anchor.snippet: is a horizontal line with y -intercept

annotations.note: The graph being a horizontal line is not stated as a Mathlib result.

annotations.label: Graph of degree 1 polynomial

anchor.snippet: is an oblique line with y -intercept

annotations.note: The graph being an oblique line is not stated as a Mathlib result.

annotations.label: Graph of degree 2 polynomial

anchor.snippet: where a 2 ≠ 0 is a parabola

annotations.note: The graph being a parabola is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Graph of degree 3 polynomial

anchor.snippet: is a cubic curve

annotations.note: The graph being a cubic curve is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Graph of degree ≥2 polynomial

anchor.snippet: is a continuous non-linear curve

annotations.note: Continuity is formalized via `Polynomial.continuous`, but the geometric "non-linear curve" description is not.

annotations.label: End behavior of polynomial functions

anchor.snippet: A non-constant polynomial function tends to infinity when the variable increases indefinitely

annotations.note: `Polynomial.tendsto_norm_atTop` shows a positive-degree polynomial's value tends to infinity.

annotations.label: Polynomial equation

anchor.snippet: A polynomial equation , also called an algebraic equation , is an equation of the form

annotations.note: Solutions of `P = 0` are captured by `Polynomial.IsRoot`/`roots`, though the equation as an object is not separately defined.

annotations.label: Unknowns, solutions, polynomial identity

anchor.snippet: the indeterminates (variables) of polynomials are also called unknowns

annotations.note: These are terminological distinctions with no dedicated Mathlib declaration.

annotations.label: Abel–Ruffini theorem

anchor.snippet: the Abel–Ruffini theorem asserts that there can not exist a general formula in radicals

annotations.note: Mathlib proves the Abel–Ruffini direction: a solvable-by-radicals root forces a solvable Galois group.

annotations.label: Fundamental theorem of algebra (number of solutions)

anchor.snippet: The number of solutions of a polynomial equation with real coefficients may not exceed the degree

annotations.note: `Polynomial.card_roots'` shows the multiset of roots has cardinality at most the degree.

annotations.label: Root of a polynomial

anchor.snippet: A root of a nonzero univariate polynomial P is a value a of x

annotations.note: `Polynomial.IsRoot p a` is defined as `p.eval a = 0`.

annotations.label: Factor theorem and multiplicity

anchor.snippet: A number a is a root of a polynomial P if and only if the linear polynomial x − a divides P

annotations.note: `Polynomial.dvd_iff_isRoot : X - C a ∣ p ↔ IsRoot p a` is the factor theorem; multiplicity is `Polynomial.rootMultiplicity`.

annotations.label: Vieta's formulas

anchor.snippet: are related by Vieta's formulas

annotations.note: `Multiset.prod_X_add_C_eq_sum_esymm` expresses coefficients of a product of linear factors via elementary symmetric polynomials.

annotations.label: Existence of complex roots

anchor.snippet: do not have any roots among the real numbers

annotations.note: `Complex.isAlgClosed` (FTA) ensures every non-constant complex polynomial has a complex root.

annotations.label: Golden ratio as a root

anchor.snippet: is the unique positive solution of

annotations.note: `Real.goldenRatio_sq : φ ^ 2 = φ + 1` states φ is a root of `x² - x - 1`.

annotations.label: Numerical approximation of roots

anchor.snippet: to compute numerical approximations of the solutions

annotations.note: Mathlib has no general numerical root-finding (e.g. Newton iteration for polynomial roots) algorithm.

annotations.label: Zeros of multivariate polynomials

anchor.snippet: the combinations of values for the variables for which the polynomial function takes the value zero

annotations.note: Vanishing sets are definable via `MvPolynomial.eval`, but no dedicated affine-variety zero-set definition exists for points.

annotations.label: System of linear equations

anchor.snippet: The special case where all the polynomials are of degree one is called a system of linear equations

annotations.note: Linear systems are handled via matrices/`LinearMap`, but there is no named "system of linear equations" object.

annotations.label: Diophantine equation

anchor.snippet: is called a Diophantine equation

annotations.note: `Dioph` formalizes Diophantine sets (integer solution sets of polynomial systems), not a single equation definition.

annotations.label: Trigonometric polynomial

anchor.snippet: A trigonometric polynomial is a finite linear combination of functions sin( nx ) and cos( nx )

annotations.note: No `TrigonometricPolynomial` definition exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Trigonometric polynomial as polynomial in sin and cos

anchor.snippet: a trigonometric polynomial becomes a polynomial in the two variables sin( x ) and cos( x )

annotations.note: This equivalence is not stated, since trigonometric polynomials are not defined.

annotations.label: Trigonometric polynomial and Fourier series

anchor.snippet: there is no difference between such a function and a finite Fourier series

annotations.note: Fourier series exist (`fourier`), but the identification with trigonometric polynomials is not formalized.

annotations.label: Matrix polynomial

anchor.snippet: A matrix polynomial is a polynomial with square matrices as variables.

annotations.note: `matPolyEquiv` identifies matrices of polynomials with polynomials having matrix coefficients; evaluation at a matrix is `Polynomial.aeval`.

annotations.label: Matrix polynomial equation and identity

anchor.snippet: A matrix polynomial equation is an equality between two matrix polynomials

annotations.note: Matrix-polynomial identities like Cayley–Hamilton exist, but no general "matrix polynomial equation" definition is given.

annotations.label: Exponential polynomial

anchor.snippet: A bivariate polynomial where the second variable is substituted for an exponential function

annotations.note: No exponential-polynomial definition exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Rational fraction and rational function

anchor.snippet: A rational fraction is the quotient ( algebraic fraction ) of two polynomials.

annotations.note: `RatFunc K` is the field of quotients of polynomials.

annotations.label: Domain of a rational function

anchor.snippet: a rational function is defined only for the values of the variables for which the denominator is not zero

annotations.note: `RatFunc` exists, but the pointwise domain (where the denominator is nonzero) is not formalized as a result.

annotations.label: Laurent polynomial

anchor.snippet: Laurent polynomials are like polynomials, but allow negative powers of the variable(s) to occur.

annotations.note: `LaurentPolynomial R` (`R[T;T⁻¹]`) allows negative powers of the variable.

annotations.label: Formal power series

anchor.snippet: Formal power series are like polynomials, but allow infinitely many non-zero terms to occur

annotations.note: `PowerSeries R` allows infinitely many nonzero coefficients.

annotations.label: Polynomial ring

anchor.snippet: A polynomial f over a commutative ring R is a polynomial all of whose coefficients belong to R .

annotations.note: `Polynomial R` is the polynomial ring over a (semi)ring `R`.

annotations.label: Multivariate reduces to iterated univariate

anchor.snippet: most of the theory of the multivariate case can be reduced to an iterated univariate case

annotations.note: `MvPolynomial.finSuccEquiv` gives `MvPolynomial (Fin (n+1)) R ≃ₐ (MvPolynomial (Fin n) R)[X]`, the iterated reduction.

annotations.label: R as subring of R[x]

anchor.snippet: is an injective ring homomorphism , by which R is viewed as a subring of R [ x ]

annotations.note: `Polynomial.C_injective` proves the constant-coefficient ring hom `C : R → R[X]` is injective.

annotations.label: Polynomials vs polynomial functions over rings

anchor.snippet: different polynomials may give rise to the same polynomial function

annotations.note: `Polynomial.funext` proves the converse over infinite domains; the distinctness over finite rings is not separately formalized.

annotations.label: Divisibility of polynomials

anchor.snippet: it is said that f divides g or f is a divisor of g if there exists a polynomial q

annotations.note: Divisibility on `R[X]` is the monoid relation `∣` characterized by `dvd_def : a ∣ b ↔ ∃ c, b = a * c`.

annotations.label: Euclidean division; F[x] is a Euclidean domain

anchor.snippet: shows that the ring F [ x ] is a Euclidean domain

annotations.note: `Polynomial.instEuclideanDomain` makes `F[X]` a `EuclideanDomain` for a field `F`.

annotations.label: Irreducible polynomials

anchor.snippet: irreducible polynomials ) can be defined as non-zero polynomials which cannot be factorized

annotations.note: The general `Irreducible` predicate applies to elements of `R[X]`.

annotations.label: Eisenstein's criterion

anchor.snippet: Eisenstein's criterion can also be used in some cases to determine irreducibility.

annotations.note: `Polynomial.IsEisensteinAt.irreducible` gives Eisenstein's criterion: an Eisenstein polynomial is irreducible.

annotations.label: Unique base representation

anchor.snippet: every positive integer a can be expressed uniquely in the form

annotations.note: `Nat.ofDigits_digits` gives the base-`b` representation (with `digits_inj_iff`/`digits_lt_base` for uniqueness and bounds).

annotations.label: Polynomial interpolation (Lagrange)

anchor.snippet: Practical methods of approximation include polynomial interpolation

annotations.note: `Lagrange.interpolate` constructs the unique polynomial of bounded degree passing through given points.

annotations.label: Taylor's theorem

anchor.snippet: Taylor's theorem , which roughly states that every differentiable function locally looks like a polynomial function

annotations.note: `taylor_mean_remainder` is Taylor's theorem with remainder term.

annotations.label: Stone–Weierstrass theorem

anchor.snippet: the Stone–Weierstrass theorem , which states that every continuous function defined on a compact interval

annotations.note: `polynomialFunctions_closure_eq_top` shows polynomial functions are dense in continuous functions on `[a,b]`.

annotations.label: Characteristic polynomial

anchor.snippet: the characteristic polynomial of a matrix or linear operator contains information about the operator's eigenvalues

annotations.note: `Matrix.charpoly` (and `LinearMap.charpoly`) defines the characteristic polynomial.

annotations.label: Minimal polynomial

anchor.snippet: the minimal polynomial of an algebraic element records the simplest algebraic relation

annotations.note: `minpoly A x` is the minimal polynomial of an algebraic element `x`.

annotations.label: Chromatic polynomial

anchor.snippet: the chromatic polynomial of a graph counts the number of proper colourings of that graph

annotations.note: No chromatic-polynomial definition exists in Mathlib (only chromatic number and colorings).

annotations.label: Polynomial time

anchor.snippet: the phrase polynomial time means that the time it takes to complete an algorithm is bounded by a polynomial function

annotations.note: Computational complexity classes such as polynomial time are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Sum of polynomials is a polynomial

anchor.snippet: When polynomials are added together, the result is another polynomial.

annotations.note: Closure of polynomials under addition is the additive structure on `R[X]`.

annotations.label: Product of polynomials is a polynomial

anchor.snippet: the product of polynomials is always a polynomial

annotations.note: Closure of polynomials under multiplication is the multiplicative structure on `R[X]`.

annotations.label: Composition of polynomials is a polynomial

anchor.snippet: The composition of two polynomials is another polynomial.

annotations.note: `Polynomial.comp p q : R[X]` lives in the polynomial ring, so composition is closed.

annotations.label: Multiplicity (root multiplicity)

anchor.snippet: which is called the multiplicity of a as a root of P

annotations.note: `Polynomial.rootMultiplicity a p` is the largest `m` with `(X - C a) ^ m ∣ p`.

annotations.label: Roots-with-multiplicity equals degree over an algebraically closed field

anchor.snippet: the number of (complex) roots counted with their multiplicities is exactly equal to the degree of the polynomial

annotations.note: `Polynomial.Splits.natDegree_eq_card_roots` shows for a splitting polynomial the multiset of roots has cardinality equal to the degree (FTA gives this over `ℂ`).

annotations.label: Polynomial evaluation at a matrix (aeval)

anchor.snippet: this polynomial evaluated at a matrix A is

annotations.note: `Polynomial.aeval A p` evaluates a scalar polynomial at a matrix (or any algebra element), substituting `I` for the constant.
